sourceFiles.name: thermal-conflict

fix.title: Bind emergency shutdown threshold attribute to the authoritative requirement
fix.description: This fix removes the redundancy risk by keeping the requirement as the authoritative obligation while explicitly documenting the CoolingSystem threshold attribute as the design realization of that requirement. The requirement is given a formal threshold attribute, and the structural attribute is bound to that requirement value through a derived expression so the two definitions cannot silently diverge.

changes.description: Made the EmergencyShutdown requirement the authoritative source of the threshold by adding a formal requirement attribute and parameterizing the requirement text with it.
Changed the CoolingSystem emergency shutdown threshold from an independently duplicated literal to a value derived from the requirement, creating explicit traceability and preventing divergence.

results.name: 04_redundancy
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: A thermal protection/control model defining when a system should shut down, activate a cooling fan, and activate sprinklers based on temperature thresholds.
- Operational domain: Industrial / thermal safety control.
- Model scope: Small model fragment containing requirement definitions and structural part definitions with threshold attributes.
- Known scope limitations: No behavioural model is provided (no state machines, actions, transitions, events, or verification cases). Relationship-level checks (`satisfy`, `allocate`, `connect`, `bind`, specialization chains) and most behaviour redundancy checks can only be assessed for explicit absence, not deep consistency.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Duplicate Requirements  -  The three requirements have distinct texts and thresholds (80C, 85C, 90C); no copied or near-identical requirement statements were found.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Duplicate Block or Part Definitions  -  `CoolingSystem`, `SprinklerSystem`, and `System` represent distinct concepts with non-identical content; no duplicated block/interface definitions were found.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Redundant Relationships  -  No explicit `satisfy`, `allocate`, `connect`, `bind`, or specialization relationships are present, so no duplicate relationships were found in this fragment.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Redundant Behaviour Models  -  No state machines, action definitions, sequences, or message exchanges are present, so there is no duplicated behaviour model content to assess.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-001]
Element(s):     thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::emergencyShutdownThresholdC
Check:          5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints
Finding:        The emergency shutdown threshold is defined twice: once in the requirement text and again as a structural attribute value. The model provides no derivation, binding, or traceability relation showing that the attribute is an implementation of the requirement rather than a duplicated restatement.
Evidence:       Requirement text: `The system shall enter emergency shutdown when temperature reaches 80 degrees Celsius.` Attribute: `attribute emergencyShutdownThresholdC : Real = 80.0;`
Score:          0.75  -  Minor-to-moderate redundancy risk because duplicated threshold values can diverge during maintenance even though they are currently consistent.
Confidence:     HIGH
Risk:           If one threshold is updated and the other is not, the model can silently encode conflicting shutdown criteria, leading to incorrect analysis or implementation decisions.
Recommendation: Keep the requirement as the authoritative source for the obligation and either derive/bind the threshold attribute explicitly from it or document the attribute as the design realisation of that requirement.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-002]
Element(s):     thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::coolingFanStartThresholdC
Check:          5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints
Finding:        The cooling fan activation threshold is duplicated between a requirement statement and a part attribute value, with no explicit refinement, satisfy, or binding relation to justify the repeated numeric definition.
Evidence:       Requirement text: `The cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 85 degrees Celsius.` Attribute: `attribute coolingFanStartThresholdC : Real = 85.0;`
Score:          0.75  -  Minor-to-moderate redundancy risk because the same threshold is restated in two places without controlled linkage.
Confidence:     HIGH
Risk:           Later edits may change the requirement or the attribute independently, producing inconsistent fan activation criteria across the model.
Recommendation: Retain the requirement and connect the design attribute to it through an explicit trace/redefinition/binding mechanism, or centralise the threshold in one authoritative definition.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-003]
Element(s):     thermal_system::SprinklerActivation; thermal_system::SprinklerSystem::activationThresholdC
Check:          5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints
Finding:        The sprinkler activation threshold is represented both as requirement text and as an attribute value, but the model does not show any formal linkage indicating intentional reuse rather than duplicated restatement.
Evidence:       Requirement text: `The sprinkler system shall turn on when temperature reaches 90 degrees Celsius.` Attribute: `attribute activationThresholdC : Real = 90.0;`
Score:          0.75  -  Minor-to-moderate redundancy risk because repeated constants can diverge over time if not linked.
Confidence:     HIGH
Risk:           Divergence between requirement and design values could invalidate safety analysis or verification expectations for sprinkler activation.
Recommendation: Keep one authoritative threshold source and relate the other by explicit derivation, traceability, or value binding; avoid unmanaged duplication of the same constant.

CHECK PASSED: 6. Redundant Decomposition  -  The decomposition is minimal and meaningful: `System` contains two distinct subsystems, and there are no empty packages or single-child pass-through blocks.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Unintentional vs. Intentional Redundancy  -  No duplicated architectural elements suggesting intentional fault-tolerance redundancy were found; the identified issues are model-information restatements, not physical redundancy.

CHECK PASSED: 8. States that are never activated  -  Although the thresholds suggest fan activation at 85C may be pre-empted by shutdown at 80C, this check cannot be confirmed in this model because no behavioural semantics or state activation logic are modelled.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| REDUNDANCY-001 | 0.75 | HIGH | Emergency shutdown threshold is duplicated in a requirement and an attribute without explicit linkage. |
| REDUNDANCY-002 | 0.75 | HIGH | Cooling fan threshold is duplicated in a requirement and an attribute without explicit linkage. |
| REDUNDANCY-003 | 0.75 | HIGH | Sprinkler threshold is duplicated in a requirement and an attribute without explicit linkage. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.75  
Model Status: Notable gaps  -  address before finalising design decisions
